title: Numbers 11-19 and Plurals in Arabic: Grammatical Structures and Usage
summary: The session focuses on teaching numerical expressions from 11 to 19 in Modern Standard Arabic, emphasizing gender agreement rules for nouns modified by these numbers. The teacher explains how the first part of composite numbers (e.g., 11-19) follows specific grammatical patterns and provides examples with different nouns like years (sana), pages (safha), and doctors (tabiba). Additionally, the lesson touches on plural formations for certain Arabic words, distinguishing between masculine and feminine plurals.
key_points: Numbers 11-19 (e.g., tisa'a 'ashra) require both parts to agree in gender with the noun they modify. For example, if modifying a female noun like sana (years), both parts take fatha: tisa'atun 'ashratin sana'tan.
Composite numbers from 11-19 use the structure [number] + ashra. The first number (e.g., tisa') is in the singular, and ashra agrees in gender with the noun being counted.
The plural forms of certain nouns (e.g., ṭulabu plurals of ṭullāb) follow specific patterns like jawan > jiyūn. Students practice deriving these plurals from given examples.
Differentiating between masculine and feminine plurals is crucial. For instance, kasalun (masculine plural: kusala) vs. kasalan (feminine).
Using the definite article in composite numbers: arba'atun 'ashratan sana'tan (19 years) instead of alif-lam.
The structure bi-kam introduces questions about price, e.g., Bi kam hazal kursī? (How much is this chair?).
